name: Tree of Thoughts (ToT) / Graph of Thoughts (GoT) — Multi-Path Reasoning with LLMs
origin_domain: AI research (large language model reasoning and search)
year_developed: Tree of Thoughts: 2023 (arXiv:2305.10601, May 2023; published at NeurIPS 2023 as Oral). Graph of Thoughts: 2023 (arXiv:2308.09687, August 2023; latest revision February 2024). Related topologies survey: 2024 (arXiv:2401.14295, Besta et al., 'Topologies of Reasoning').
progenitor: Tree of Thoughts: Shunyu Yao, Dian Yu, Jeffrey Zhao, Izhak Shafran, Thomas L. Griffiths, Yuan Cao, Karthik Narasimhan (Princeton University and Google DeepMind). Shunyu Yao is also first author of ReAct. Graph of Thoughts: Maciej Besta, Nils Blach, Ales Kubicek, Robert Gerstenberger, Lukas Gianinazzi, Joanna Gajda, Tomasz Lehmann, Michal Podstawski, Hubert Niewiadomski, Piotr Nyczyk, Torsten Hoefler (ETH Zurich, Cledar, Warsaw University of Technology).
driving_insight: Standard Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and even ReAct follow a single reasoning path — the model commits to one line of thinking and follows it to completion. This is brittle: if the initial direction is wrong, the entire trajectory is wasted. Humans solving hard problems do not follow a single line of reasoning — they explore multiple possibilities, evaluate which seem promising, backtrack from dead ends, and combine insights from different lines of thought. ToT operationalizes this: the LLM generates multiple candidate 'thoughts' at each step, evaluates their promise (using the LLM itself as a value function), and uses classical search algorithms (BFS/DFS) to explore the most promising paths first. GoT generalizes this further: thoughts are vertices in an arbitrary graph, not just a tree, enabling merging insights from different branches, refining thoughts through feedback loops, and distilling entire subnetworks of reasoning into compact summaries. The driving insight is that search over reasoning paths — combined with LLM-based evaluation and pruning — can dramatically improve problem-solving on tasks where the right approach is not obvious upfront, turning the LLM from a single-path reasoner into a deliberative search agent.
decision_style: algorithmic and deliberative — decisions about which reasoning paths to explore, which to prune, and when to backtrack are made by classical search algorithms (BFS, DFS, beam search, MCTS) that are external to the LLM. The LLM provides two functions: (1) thought generation — proposing candidate next steps from a given state (a generative function); (2) state evaluation — scoring how promising each path appears (a value function). The search algorithm is the meta-decision-maker: it decides which path to expand next based on LLM-provided scores and the search strategy. This is a hybrid decision architecture: the LLM handles the content-level decisions (what thoughts to generate, how to evaluate them), while the search algorithm handles the process-level decisions (which path to explore, when to backtrack, when to stop). This separation of concerns — content generation vs. search control — is a key architectural insight and distinguishes ToT/GoT from purely LLM-driven decision architectures like ReAct.
problem_class_targeted: Complex reasoning problems where: (a) the solution space is large and the right path is not obvious upfront; (b) intermediate steps can be meaningfully evaluated for promise (you can tell if you're on the right track or a dead end); (c) the problem admits decomposition into coherent intermediate 'thoughts'; (d) backtracking is possible (you can abandon a bad path and try another). ToT was demonstrated on: mathematical puzzles (Game of 24 — combinatorial search), creative writing (generating and evaluating multiple story plans), and mini-crosswords (constrained word search). GoT was demonstrated on: sorting (algorithmic reasoning where merging partial solutions matters), set operations (intersection, union), keyword counting (text processing requiring aggregation of partial results), and document merging. The approach targets problems where CoT fails because the model commits to a wrong step early and cannot recover. Less suited to: open-ended conversation (no clear evaluation criterion), tasks where a single correct path exists and can be found with CoT, and tasks where the evaluation of intermediate states is unreliable (the LLM value function is noisy).
ordered_phases: ToT operates as a search process with four interacting components, not linear phases: (1) Thought Decomposition — define what a 'thought' is for this problem (e.g., a partial equation, a sentence, a word placement). This is done once upfront. (2) Thought Generation — from a given state (partial solution), the LLM generates k candidate next thoughts. This is repeated at each search step for the current frontier state. (3) State Evaluation — the LLM evaluates each candidate state on its promise (scalar score, classification as 'sure/likely/impossible', or comparative ranking). Evaluation can use the LLM itself (value prompting) or be heuristic. (4) Search — the search algorithm (BFS, DFS, beam) selects which state to expand next, prunes unpromising states, and backtracks when a path is exhausted. This continues until a terminal state (complete solution) is found or the search budget is exhausted. GoT adds two more operations: (5) Aggregate — combine multiple thoughts into a synthesized result (many-to-1); (6) Improve — refine a thought through self-feedback (1-to-1 recurrence). The GoT Graph of Operations (GoO) defines the allowed transformations as a directed graph structure, and the system executes these operations as specified by the GoO architecture.
feedback_loops: Multiple feedback loops at different levels: (1) Search backtracking — when a path is evaluated as unpromising, the search algorithm backtracks to an earlier state and explores an alternative branch. This is the primary feedback loop and is explicit (designed by the search algorithm). (2) State evaluation feedback — generated thoughts are scored, and low scores prune that branch, preventing further resource expenditure. This is a feed-forward loop (evaluation -> pruning) that shapes exploration. (3) GoT's Improve operation — a thought is fed back to the LLM for self-refinement, creating a local refinement loop within a single vertex. (4) GoT's Aggregate operation — multiple thoughts are combined, creating a bottom-up synthesis loop where partial results from different branches inform a merged result. In GoT, the graph structure allows arbitrary feedback — thoughts can feed into each other in any topology defined by the Graph of Operations, including cycles (though the paper primarily demonstrates acyclic graphs). The feedback is structured and algorithmic — unlike ReAct's emergent self-correction, ToT/GoT's feedback is programmatic and guaranteed by the search algorithm.
gates: The primary gate is the state evaluation at each search step: before a candidate thought is added to the search frontier, it must pass the evaluation threshold. The evaluation criteria depend on the task: (a) value scoring — thought must score above a threshold (e.g., >5/10); (b) classification — thought must be classified as 'sure' or 'likely,' not 'impossible'; (c) voting — thought must receive majority votes from multiple evaluation samples. What happens when a gate blocks: the thought is pruned — it is not added to the search frontier and no further resources are spent exploring that branch. Additional gates: (a) search budget gate — the search terminates when a maximum number of expansions is reached; (b) solution quality gate — a candidate complete solution must meet a quality threshold to be accepted (e.g., in Game of 24, the solution must evaluate to 24). These gates are explicit, designed, and enforced by the search algorithm — they are more formal than ReAct's implicit stopping conditions but less bureaucratic than MDMP's phase-gate structure.
exit_criteria: The search process terminates when: (a) a terminal state (complete, valid solution) is found — in ToT, this is a thought sequence that solves the problem; (b) the search budget is exhausted (maximum number of LLM calls or search steps reached) without finding a solution — the best partial result may be returned; (c) the search frontier is empty (all paths have been explored or pruned) — no solution found. Multiple solutions can be found — the search can continue after finding the first solution to find a better one (best-first search). Partial completion: intermediate states in the search tree represent partial solutions that may be useful even if a complete solution is not found. GoT's exit criteria additionally include: the distilled/aggregated output meets a quality threshold, or the graph of operations has been fully executed.
decomposition_method: The decomposition is problem-specific and designed by the human practitioner: for each problem type, the practitioner defines what constitutes a 'thought' (the atomic unit of the search) and how thoughts combine into solutions. This is manual, not automated. For Game of 24: a thought is a partial equation (e.g., '2+3=5, remaining: 4, 6'); thoughts combine linearly into a solution. For creative writing: a thought is a paragraph plan; thoughts form a tree of narrative options. For mini-crosswords: a thought is a word placement; thoughts are independent search steps. The decomposition is NOT done by the LLM — it is a human design decision that encodes domain knowledge. This is a significant difference from P&E architectures where the LLM performs the decomposition. The strength is that the decomposition is domain-optimized; the weakness is that it requires human expertise and does not generalize across problem types without re-engineering. GoT allows more complex decomposition through the Graph of Operations — the human designs the graph topology (which operations to apply in which order) to match the problem structure.
granularity_guidance: A 'thought' should be: (a) small enough that the LLM can generate and evaluate it reliably in one call; (b) large enough that evaluating it provides meaningful signal about the path's promise; (c) coherent — a thought should be a self-contained reasoning unit that can be understood in isolation; (d) composable — thoughts should combine to form complete solutions. The granularity is problem-dependent and human-designed. In the paper: for Game of 24, a thought is one arithmetic operation (very fine-grained, 2-4 steps per solution); for creative writing, a thought is one paragraph plan (medium granularity, 4-5 steps); for mini-crosswords, a thought is one word placement (medium granularity, ~10 steps). The stopping rule for recursion is domain-specific: thoughts stop when the problem is solved (terminal state reached). There is no automated granularity control — the human sets the granularity through the thought definition.
boundary_rules: Thoughts are designed to be modular: each thought should have clear inputs (the current state) and outputs (the state after applying the thought). In ToT, thoughts in a path are sequential — each thought transforms the state into the next state, with no overlap between thoughts (a thought only operates on the state produced by the previous thought). Different paths in the tree are independent — they do not interact. In GoT, boundary rules are defined by the Graph of Operations: each operation specifies how many inputs it consumes and how many outputs it produces. The GoO enforces clean boundaries — an Aggregate operation, for instance, explicitly consumes N thoughts and produces 1 merged thought. However, there are no formal MECE rules — different branches may redundantly explore similar reasoning territory, and the system has no mechanism to detect or prevent this. The boundaries are structural (enforced by the graph topology) but not semantic (the system does not check whether two branches are solving overlapping sub-problems).
risk_assessment_mechanism: Risk assessment is embedded in the state evaluation function: the LLM's evaluation score is implicitly a risk assessment — a high score means 'this path looks promising (low risk of being a dead end),' while a low score means 'this path looks unpromising (high risk of wasting resources).' The evaluation is LLM-judged: the model is prompted to assess the promise of a partial solution. The paper experimented with two evaluation strategies: (a) value — the model outputs a scalar score (e.g., 1-10); (b) vote — the model classifies the state as 'sure/likely/impossible' and multiple samples are aggregated by majority. The evaluation is inherently noisy — the LLM's judgment of a partial solution's promise is imperfect and may incorrectly prune good paths or waste resources on bad paths. There is no probabilistic risk scoring, no systematic risk taxonomy, and no accounting for evaluation uncertainty. The search budget (maximum LLM calls) serves as a hard risk limit — the approach will not spend more than N calls regardless of promise.
unknown_handling: ToT/GoT handle unknowns through exploration: if the LLM is uncertain about which next step is best, the search algorithm explores multiple candidates in parallel and lets subsequent evaluation determine which was correct. This is 'probe and evaluate' rather than 'resolve then act.' The search algorithm's backtracking capability means that pursuing a wrong path (acting on uncertain grounds) is not catastrophic — the system can backtrack and try alternatives. However, the handling of unknowns depends entirely on the quality of the state evaluation function: if the evaluation cannot distinguish promising from unpromising paths (i.e., the LLM has no useful judgment about the unknown), search degrades to random exploration. The approach does not escalate unknowns to humans — it exhausts its search budget autonomously. For truly novel situations where the LLM has no basis for evaluation, ToT/GoT has no mechanism to recognize its own ignorance and would continue exploring randomly until the budget is exhausted.
default_stance_on_unknown: PERMISSIVE — the system explores uncertain paths rather than halting. Multiple candidate thoughts are generated, and evaluation prunes the worst rather than requiring confidence before proceeding. This permissive-exploratory stance is well-suited to the problem class (complex reasoning where the right path is uncertain) — it is the entire point of the approach. However, the permissiveness is bounded by the search budget: the system will not explore indefinitely; after exhausting its budget, it returns the best result found or no result. The permissive stance is safer here than in tool-use agents because thoughts are purely linguistic — generating and evaluating a bad thought costs tokens but does not take irreversible actions.
safety_guardrails: Minimal safety concerns because ToT/GoT operates in a purely linguistic reasoning space — there are no tools, no code execution, no external actions. The primary safety guardrails are: (a) the search budget caps resource consumption (prevents infinite loops or runaway token spend); (b) the evaluation function prevents obviously bad reasoning from propagating (though miss-evaluation is a risk); (c) the non-destructive nature of thoughts means a wrong evaluation only wastes tokens, not causes harm. In extended implementations where ToT/GoT is combined with tool use (e.g., Language Agent Tree Search), additional guardrails are required: sandboxed tool execution, allowlisting of permissible actions, and human approval for high-stakes actions. The original ToT/GoT papers do not address adversarial risks — prompt injection through malicious problem statements, or the LLM generating harmful reasoning paths. In principle, the same indirect prompt injection vulnerabilities that affect other LLM architectures apply here, but the search-bounded nature limits exposure.
human_decision_points: The original ToT/GoT papers are fully autonomous — no human decision points are designed into the architecture. Humans define the thought structure and search algorithm upfront (meta-level design decisions), but the search process itself runs without human intervention. In principle, humans could be inserted at: (a) solution review — the human evaluates the final output before accepting it; (b) search steering — a human observes the search progress and can redirect exploration (prune a branch the system thought was promising but the human knows is wrong, or expand a branch the system pruned); (c) dead-end diagnosis — when the search exhausts its budget without finding a solution, a human can analyze the search tree to identify why and provide guidance for a restarted search. These human-in-the-loop extensions are straightforward to implement but are not part of the published methods. The human is OFF the loop by default in ToT/GoT — the system operates autonomously.
override_capability: No built-in override mechanism in the original papers. In principle, the search process can be paused at any time, and a human can: (a) inject new thoughts into the frontier; (b) remove thoughts from the frontier; (c) change the evaluation scores on specific states; (d) redirect the search to a different region of the tree; (e) terminate the search early and accept the best solution found so far. The search tree structure makes override practical — a human can point to specific nodes and say 'expand this' or 'prune that.' The granularity of override is per-state (per node in the search tree). Compared to ReAct (where the trajectory is a linear sequence), the tree structure enables more surgical intervention.
fallback_when_no_human: The system is designed for fully autonomous operation — no human is required. When the search budget is exhausted without finding a solution, the system returns the best partial result found or indicates failure. There is no escalation mechanism — the system does not request human help when stuck. The fallback is graceful degradation: the search produces the best answer it could find within the budget, which may be suboptimal or incomplete, but the system does not block indefinitely. For production use, adding a 'request human assistance' action when search is exhausted would be a natural extension.
max_problem_size: ToT was demonstrated on: Game of 24 (combinatorial search, 4 input numbers, branching factor ~5-10 per step, depth 2-4), Creative Writing (paragraph planning, branching factor ~5, depth 4-5), Mini-Crosswords (5x5 grid, ~10 word placements). GoT was demonstrated on: Sorting (up to 128 numbers using parallel merge with GoT vs. 32 for ToT), Set Operations (intersection/union of up to 128 elements), Keyword Counting (document-level text processing). The theoretical limit is determined by: (a) search space size — the branching factor multiplied by search depth determines the number of states that must be explored; (b) LLM call budget — each state expansion requires one or more LLM calls; (c) value function reliability — as the search space grows, the evaluation function's noise increasingly degrades search efficiency. What breaks at scale: (1) the LLM evaluation function becomes unreliable for deep partial solutions (it's hard to judge the promise of a state that is many steps from completion); (2) token cost grows with the search tree size (potentially exponential in depth for BFS); (3) the context window must hold the entire search history for the current path. GoT's aggregation and distillation operations partially mitigate scale by compressing subgraphs into compact summaries, but the fundamental scaling is exponential in the branching factor.
parallel_execution: ToT supports parallelism at multiple levels: (a) thought generation — multiple candidate thoughts from the same state can be generated in parallel (independent LLM calls); (b) state evaluation — multiple states can be evaluated in parallel; (c) BFS naturally exposes parallelism — all states at the current depth can be expanded simultaneously. GoT adds: (d) parallel subgraph execution — independent subgraphs of the GoO can be executed concurrently. Parallelism is first-class: the search algorithms naturally produce independent work units (candidate thoughts, frontier states) that can be executed in parallel. The paper does not implement parallel execution (all experiments are sequential), but the architecture is inherently parallelizable. Dependency resolution is handled by the search algorithm: a state cannot be expanded until it has been generated and evaluated. GoT's Graph of Operations explicitly models dependencies — operations are only executed when all their input vertices are available.
team_scaling_model: Not directly addressed in the original papers — ToT/GoT are single-LLM architectures where one model generates and evaluates all thoughts. Multi-agent extensions are conceptually straightforward: (a) different LLMs could generate candidate thoughts (diverse perspectives), with a controller LLM or algorithm selecting among them; (b) specialized evaluator LLMs could score thoughts in parallel; (c) different agents could explore different branches of the search tree concurrently. Scaling with more agents is approximately linear for independent exploration (more agents = more branches explored in parallel = faster coverage of the search space), but communication overhead grows if the agents need to share their findings (e.g., to avoid redundant exploration). The GoT model is more amenable to multi-agent scaling because the graph structure explicitly models information flow between operations, which can be distributed across agents.
cost_scaling_model: Token cost is proportional to the number of LLM calls: cost = O(states_generated + states_evaluated). For BFS with branching factor b and depth d: states generated = O(b^d), making worst-case cost exponential in depth. However, evaluation-based pruning dramatically reduces the effective branching factor — in practice, b_effective << b because unpromising branches are pruned. ToT's Game of 24 experiments used b=5-10 but effective exploration was much narrower. Beam search (ToT paper's preferred variant) caps the frontier size to beam_width w, bounding cost to O(w * d). GoT's cost is: O(|V| + |E|) where V is vertices (thoughts) and E is edges (operations), allowing more efficient scaling than ToT for problems where aggregation and distillation reduce the vertex count. The GoT paper reports 31%+ cost reduction compared to ToT on sorting tasks because aggregation operations compress multiple partial results into single representations. The cost scaling is fundamentally better than ToT's exponential worst-case because the graph structure allows the LLM to combine and compress intermediate results. The branching factor varies: in ToT, it's the number of candidate thoughts per state (typically 5-10); in GoT, the GoO defines the branching via the operation types (Generate is 1-to-many, others are many-to-1 or 1-to-1). Cost is stable within a search budget but can spike if the evaluation function is poorly calibrated and fails to prune effectively.
replanning_built_in: Replanning is not a separate phase — it is the search algorithm itself. Backtracking IS replanning: when a path proves unpromising, the search abandons it and explores alternatives. This is continuous, algorithmic replanning at the per-state level. The search algorithm replans locally — it only backtracks from the specific state that proved unpromising, not the entire solution attempt. Global replanning (restarting the entire search) would only occur if the human changes the problem specification or thought definition. The search architecture means replanning is neither an afterthought nor a first-class phase — it is intrinsic to the search process. This is a key distinction from P&E architectures where replanning is an explicit, separate step.
trigger_mechanism: Replanning (backtracking) is triggered by the evaluation function: when a state's evaluated promise falls below threshold, the search algorithm backtracks from that state. Triggers are: (a) low evaluation score — the LLM judges the state as unlikely to lead to a solution; (b) depth limit reached — the path has not produced a solution within the maximum depth; (c) search budget exhausted for that branch — a per-branch budget cap is reached. Additionally: (d) in BFS, the algorithm may backtrack from a state NOT because it's bad but because another state is evaluated as MORE promising — this is priority-based backtracking. The triggers are explicit, algorithmic, and deterministic given the evaluation scores and search parameters. This is more reliable than ReAct's emergent replanning but depends critically on evaluation function accuracy.
adaptation_cost: Backtracking cost is the wasted LLM calls on the abandoned path plus the cost of generating/evaluating new candidate thoughts. The wasted cost depends on search strategy: BFS with pruning minimizes wasted deep exploration (shallow pruning); DFS risks deep wasted exploration (may explore a long path before discovering it's a dead end). Beam search balances these. The cost of switching to an alternative path is: generating and evaluating new candidate thoughts at the backtrack point — typically 1-2 LLM calls per alternative. State preservation: the search tree structure preserves all explored states, so switching paths does not lose information — the alternate path can reference earlier partial solutions if needed (GoT's graph structure explicitly supports this). The adaptation cost is bounded by the search budget — the system will not exceed its maximum LLM call count regardless of how many times it backtracks.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Failure recovery is the search algorithm itself — backtracking from failed paths and exploring alternatives. The mechanism is: (1) Detection — the evaluation function identifies an unpromising state (failure imminent) or the state cannot be expanded (dead end — no valid candidate thoughts from this state). (2) Isolation — the search algorithm marks the failed state as explored/exhausted so it is not revisited. (3) Recovery — the algorithm selects the next most promising state from the frontier (best-first) or backtracks to the parent and tries a sibling (DFS). (4) Containment — the failure of one branch does not affect other branches (the search tree structure naturally contains blast radius). In GoT, additional recovery mechanisms exist: (a) if an Aggregate operation fails (merged thoughts are incoherent), the system can try different combinations; (b) if an Improve operation fails (refinement degrades quality), the system can retain the original thought; (c) the graph structure allows partial results to be salvaged and routed to different operations. The search budget caps the number of recovery attempts — after exhausting the budget, the system reports failure.
output_artifacts: The search process produces: (a) the Search Tree/DAG — the complete record of all states explored, with evaluation scores, parent-child relationships, and terminal states; (b) the Solution Path — the sequence of thoughts from root to terminal state that constitutes the found solution; (c) Alternative Solutions — if multiple terminal states were found, ranked by quality; (d) Search Statistics — number of states generated, explored, pruned, LLM calls consumed, budget remaining. The search tree is the primary artifact — it provides a complete trace of the reasoning exploration process, including paths NOT taken and why. This is more information-rich than a ReAct trajectory (which shows only the path taken) or a P&E plan (which shows the intended path but not alternatives considered). The GoT paper additionally produces: (e) the Graph of Operations specification — the designed computation topology; (f) the executed graph — the instantiated vertices and edges with their values.
briefing_format: The search tree is self-documenting for practitioners who understand search algorithms — it shows what was explored and why. For non-technical stakeholders, the briefing format is the solution path with explanation: 'The problem was solved by exploring X paths, of which Y were evaluated as promising. The solution found: [solution]. Alternative considered but rejected: [alternatives].' The ToT paper presents results as the solution with supporting search statistics. The GoT paper uses the Graph of Operations diagram as the briefing artifact — it visually communicates the computation structure. For agent systems, the briefing would be: the complete solution with a summary of the search process (paths explored, confidence score, alternatives considered). The richness of the search tree as an audit artifact is a ToT/GoT advantage — you can inspect not just what the system decided but what alternatives it considered and rejected.
traceability: Strong traceability: every step in the solution path traces back to the search algorithm's decisions (why this state was expanded, what its evaluation score was, why it was selected over alternatives). The search tree provides a complete decision record: which state was chosen for expansion (because it had the highest evaluation score), what candidates were generated (the LLM's proposals), how they were evaluated (scores and rationale), and which were pruned (below threshold). This is significantly stronger than ReAct's traceability (which only shows the path taken) because the search tree preserves alternatives and their evaluations. However, the LLM's internal reasoning for evaluation scores is not always captured — the model outputs a score but may not articulate its full rationale. The paper's 'vote' strategy with multiple samples provides some visibility: you can see inter-sample agreement/disagreement, which indicates evaluation confidence.
agent_communication_protocol: Not applicable in the original single-LLM papers. In multi-agent ToT/GoT extensions: (a) candidate thought generation can be distributed across multiple LLM agents, with each agent proposing thoughts from a different perspective; (b) evaluation can be performed by a separate evaluator agent or ensemble; (c) the search frontier is a shared data structure — agents consume states from the frontier and produce new states to add to it. The communication pattern is producer-consumer: thought generator agents produce candidates, evaluator agents score them, and the search controller (algorithmic) routes states. This maps to a shared workspace model where the search tree/frontier is the shared state. MCP or ACP would serve as the serialization layer for state representations and thought content.
learning_memory_architecture: No built-in learning or persistent memory — each problem is solved from scratch with no knowledge transfer between problems. The search tree is built fresh for each problem instance. Extensions that add learning: (a) Cached Evaluations — store evaluation patterns for similar states across problems to avoid recomputation; (b) Learned Heuristics — train a value function (e.g., fine-tune a smaller model) on successful search trees to improve evaluation quality and reduce LLM call cost; (c) Plan Library — store successful solution paths for problem types and use them as starting points or heuristics for similar problems. Language Agent Tree Search (LATS, Zhou et al., 2024) adds memory by storing successful action-observation sequences from previous trials. The lack of cross-problem learning is a significant limitation — the system cannot improve its search strategy over time based on experience.
latency_bounds: Not suitable for real-time or interactive use in the general case — search over reasoning paths requires multiple LLM calls, each taking seconds. Serial latency is the number of sequential LLM calls on the critical path (search depth d = O(d) calls, each 2-10 seconds). For Game of 24 (depth 2-4, beam width 3-5), total time was tens of seconds to a few minutes. For GoT sorting (depth ~log n, parallel branches), total time depends on the GoO topology. Parallel execution of independent branches reduces wall-clock time but not total LLM call count. Under time pressure: (a) reduce beam width (more aggressive pruning, faster but riskier); (b) reduce search depth (accept coarser solutions); (c) use smaller/faster models for evaluation (e.g., GPT-3.5 for evaluation, GPT-4 for generation); (d) cap the total number of LLM calls. The architecture degrades gracefully — shallower search with narrower beams produces lower-quality but faster results. However, for very tight time budgets (seconds), ToT/GoT is inappropriate — CoT or single-pass generation is the only option.
evaluation_benchmarking: ToT was evaluated on: (1) Game of 24 — GPT-4 with ToT achieved 74% success rate vs. 7.3% for standard prompting and 4.0% for CoT (measured by task success rate). (2) Creative Writing — human evaluators preferred ToT over CoT 41:21 in blind pairwise comparison (measured by human preference). (3) Mini-Crosswords (5x5) — ToT achieved 20% word-level success vs. <10% for baselines; 60% letter-level success rate; 4/20 full puzzles solved. GoT was evaluated on: (1) Sorting — up to 128 numbers, with 62% quality improvement over ToT and >31% cost reduction (measured by output correctness and token cost). (2) Set Operations — intersection and union of document sets, with quality improvements over baselines. (3) Keyword Counting — GoT matched or exceeded ToT quality with lower cost. (4) Document Merging — GoT produced higher-quality merges than baselines. Models: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, PaLM 2. Both papers used GPT-4 for primary evaluation. The metrics were task-specific (success rate, accuracy, human preference, quality scores) rather than standardized. Neither paper evaluated on modern agent benchmarks (SWE-bench, GAIA, WebArena). Validation was limited to relatively small, controlled problem instances.
mapping_strength: Moderate-to-strong mapping to JORDAN's planning and decision-making nodes. ToT/GoT's search-over-reasoning pattern maps to: (a) the Plan node generating multiple candidate plans (thought generation) and evaluating them (state evaluation) before selecting one for execution; (b) the Research node exploring multiple information-seeking paths and pruning unpromising directions; (c) the Replan node evaluating alternative recovery strategies when the primary plan fails. The search-and-evaluate pattern is a natural fit for JORDAN's deliberative decision-making — where JORDAN currently generates a single plan (P&E style), ToT suggests generating multiple candidate plans and using systematic evaluation and search to select the best. What transfers directly: multi-path plan generation, LLM-based plan evaluation, and search-based backtracking when plans fail. What needs translation: ToT/GoT were designed for pure reasoning tasks with no external actions — extending to tool-using agents requires incorporating action-outcome feedback into the evaluation function.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN: (1) No external action model — ToT/GoT operate in a closed linguistic world; JORDAN needs search-based reasoning combined with tool use and environment interaction. (2) Human-designed thought decomposition — ToT requires a human to define what a 'thought' is for each problem type; JORDAN needs automated decomposition for arbitrary tasks. (3) No persistent memory across problems — each task is solved independently; JORDAN needs the ability to reuse successful reasoning patterns across missions. (4) Evaluation function is noisy and unreliable — LLM-based evaluation of partial reasoning states is imperfect and can mislead the search; for high-stakes JORDAN tasks, unreliable evaluation is dangerous. (5) Cost scales poorly with problem size — worst-case exponential cost in search depth makes ToT impractical for deep, complex tasks without aggressive pruning (which risks missing the solution). (6) No handling of dynamic environments — ToT assumes a static problem; JORDAN operates in environments where new information arrives continuously. (7) The search is over reasoning, not over actions — for JORDAN, the search needs to be over action sequences with real-world outcomes, which have different evaluation characteristics than pure reasoning. (8) Single-LLM architecture — ToT uses one model for both generation and evaluation, creating a potential self-reinforcement bias (the model may rate its own ideas highly). (9) GoT's Graph of Operations requires upfront human design — it is not automatically generated from the task. (10) No integration with structured plan representations — ToT outputs reasoning traces, not structured task plans that can be executed by worker agents.
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) Implement multi-path plan generation in the Plan node — instead of generating a single plan, generate k=3-5 candidate plans and use an evaluation LLM pass to score and select the best. This is the most direct and impactful ToT application to JORDAN. (2) Add a search-based Replan node — when a plan step fails, generate multiple recovery strategies (candidate replans), evaluate their promise, and select the best (beam-search over replanning options). (3) Use ToT-style evaluation in the Research node — when exploring multiple information sources, evaluate which leads are most promising before committing to deep investigation. (4) Implement a 'plan evaluation' verification step that scores plans on: completeness (covers all requirements), consistency (no contradictory steps), feasibility (steps are executable by available agents), and risk (probability of step failure). (5) Add search statistics to JORDAN's mission log — record how many plans were considered, which were rejected and why, and the search path that led to the executed plan. This provides auditability. (6) For tasks where the right approach is genuinely uncertain, use full ToT search (generate multiple plans, execute their first steps in sandbox, evaluate partial results, and pursue the most promising plan). (7) Implement plan caching — store successful plans and their evaluation scores in a plan library; for similar future tasks, retrieve and adapt rather than generate from scratch (reducing cost while preserving multi-path exploration for novel tasks). (8) Use beam search over plan execution — maintain a beam of top-k plans, execute one step of each, evaluate the results, and keep the most promising.
validation_context: ToT was published at NeurIPS 2023 as an Oral presentation (top-tier ML conference, ~1% oral acceptance). The paper has 1,500+ citations as of 2025. Validated on three distinct problem types (mathematical puzzles, creative writing, constrained search) with strong empirical results — particularly the 74% vs. 4% improvement on Game of 24 with GPT-4, which demonstrates the qualitative leap from single-path to multi-path reasoning. GoT was published at NeurIPS 2024 and validated on additional problem types (sorting, set operations, document merging) with demonstrated improvements over ToT in both quality and cost. Independently validated by the explosion of related work: RAP (Reasoning via Planning with MCTS), LATS (Language Agent Tree Search — unifies ToT with ReAct tool use), Boosting of Thoughts (ensemble of trial-and-error across many trees), Diagram of Thought (graph-based topological reasoning), Tree of Uncertain Thoughts (TouT, adds uncertainty quantification). The broader 'structure-enhanced LLM reasoning' paradigm has been validated across dozens of papers and multiple research groups. Limitations of original validation: small problem instances, no agent/tool-use tasks, GPT-4 dependence (performance on weaker models not established), and no real-world deployment evaluation.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) Evaluation noise — the LLM value function mis-scores states, either pruning promising paths (false negatives — the solution is missed) or exploring unpromising paths (false positives — resources wasted). The Game of 24 results show both: 26% of problems were not solved despite search, and some solved problems required significant exploration. (2) Search budget exhaustion — for hard problems, the search exhausts its budget before finding a solution, returning failure despite a solution existing in the space. (3) Shallow evaluation — for creative tasks, the LLM struggles to evaluate partial solutions meaningfully (how do you rate the promise of half a story?), making the search no better than random exploration. (4) Over-pruning — aggressive evaluation thresholds intended to save cost inadvertently prune the solution path; this is the exploration-exploitation tension in search. (5) Redundant exploration — different branches explore semantically similar reasoning territory, wasting resources; the system has no mechanism to detect and merge similar states. (6) Model quality dependence — ToT/GoT require strong LLMs (GPT-4 level) for reliable evaluation; with weaker models, evaluation quality degrades to noise, and search becomes random. The GoT paper acknowledges that GoT requires GPT-4 quality for the aggregation and improvement operations to work. (7) Domain-specific design burden — the human must design the thought representation, search strategy, and evaluation criteria for each problem type; no general-purpose ToT/GoT works across tasks. (8) Cost vs. benefit threshold — for easy problems that CoT solves correctly, ToT adds cost without benefit; the approach is only valuable when the problem is hard enough to justify the search overhead. (9) The ToT paper's creative writing evaluation showed that while human raters preferred ToT outputs to CoT, they did not prefer ToT outputs to human-written text — the approach improves LLM output but does not close the human-LLM quality gap. (10) Scalability collapse — for deep problems (depth > 5), the search space grows combinatorially and pruning becomes both necessary and dangerous (you must prune to afford the search, but pruning may remove the solution).
where_still_used: ToT and GoT are primarily used in research contexts and have influenced a generation of follow-up work: (1) Reasoning via Planning (RAP) — combines ToT-style search with Monte Carlo Tree Search for LLM reasoning, used in research at multiple universities. (2) Language Agent Tree Search (LATS) — unifies ToT with ReAct for tool-using agents, demonstrating strong results on WebShop and HotpotQA. (3) LLMCompiler (Kim et al., 2023) — applies DAG-based planning to function calling, conceptually related to GoT's DAG of operations. (4) Graph-based reasoning frameworks — Diagram of Thought (DoT), Cumulative Reasoning (CR), and Forest of Thoughts (FoT) extend the paradigm. (5) The 'Topologies of Reasoning' survey (Besta et al., 2024) provides a taxonomy used by researchers to position new work. Direct production use of ToT/GoT is limited due to cost and latency — most production LLM applications use single-path generation (CoT or ReAct). However, the PARADIGM of multi-path evaluation is increasingly adopted: many production agents internally generate multiple candidate responses and use LLM-based ranking to select the best, which is a simplified form of ToT. The approach is most impactful as a conceptual foundation — it demonstrated that search over reasoning paths works and inspired architectures that incorporate evaluation, pruning, and backtracking into agent workflows.
